source.title: Brain network dynamics are hierarchically organized in time
source.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

confidence_rationale: Empirical resting-state fMRI study using a hidden Markov model to identify transient brain states and their transition probabilities. The finding of hierarchical temporal organisation -- with fast micro-states nested inside slower macro-states -- directly supports MECH-047's claim that mode transitions are not instantaneous and carry switching costs. The hierarchical structure provides an empirical basis for distinguishing sub-mode fluctuations from genuine mode transitions. Main caveat: resting-state only; task-engaged dynamics and causal role of LC-NE are not directly addressed.

mapping.source_claim_statement: An HMM applied to resting-state fMRI (HCP dataset, N=1003) identifies 12 transient brain states with characteristic dwell times ranging from ~100 ms to several seconds. Transitions between states are non-random: some pairs of states have high transition probability (fast switching corridor), others are separated by low-probability gaps (switching barrier). The temporal structure is hierarchical: faster micro-state fluctuations are nested within slower macro-state epochs, producing multi-timescale dynamics.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-047: The non-random transition probability matrix from Vidaurre et al. is the empirical analog of the REE mode-transition gate. States that are separated by low transition probability correspond to mode pairs with high switching cost (e.g., doing <-> default-mode). The hierarchical nesting (fast micro-states within slow macro-states) directly maps to REE's distinction between within-mode fluctuations (E1/E2 updates) and genuine control-plane mode changes (E3/mode-manager level). Dwell times provide a quantitative prior for the hysteresis time constant in MECH-047.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Resting-state data: the 12 HMM states are not labelled as doing/ready-vigilance/default-mode and do not correspond to externally defined cognitive modes. The mapping from HMM states to REE modes is inferential. The paper does not address LC-NE as a transition driver, leaving the arousal-gating component of MECH-047 unsupported by this entry alone. DOI verified; citation details reliable.
mapping.source_context: Resting-state fMRI from the Human Connectome Project (HCP). HMM-based state inference on whole-brain parcellated BOLD time series. No task manipulation; no pharmacological or arousal manipulation.

confidence_components.notes: High-quality empirical paper in a top venue with large sample; mapping fidelity moderate because HMM states are not the same as named cognitive modes; transfer risk reflects resting-state-to-task-engaged gap and the absence of arousal manipulation.
